preFix.title: Disguising woodworm exit holes after treatment
preFix.subtitle: Fill and colour-match the flight holes left in timber after a woodworm infestation has been treated
preFix.excerpt: How to fill woodworm exit holes in furniture, beams, or floorboards after treatment with a coloured stopping wax or tinted filler, so that the holes are not visible at normal viewing distance.
preFix.sourceNotes: PCA (Property Care Association) woodworm treatment guidance. BRE Good Repair Guide on timber pest damage. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: , confirm that the infestation has been treated and is inactive. Active infestation produces fresh frass (pale, gritty powder around holes) and new holes each spring. Filling active holes is pointless, new holes will appear the following season. Once the infestation is confirmed inactive (no fresh frass for two full seasons, or after professional treatment), filling is a purely cosmetic operation.

Wood stopping wax sticks in a colour or colours matched to the timber (beeswax stopping sticks are available in a wide range of wood tones). Alternatively, two-part epoxy wood filler in a colour close to the wood tone, tinted with pigment if needed. Fine sandpaper, 180 to 240 grit. Clear finishing oil, wax polish, or the existing finish to recoat the area after filling.

Vacuum all woodworm holes and the timber surface around them to remove frass. Frass left in the holes will prevent the filler from seating. For holes that have been treated with a surface preservative, wipe the surface with a clean dry cloth to remove any oily residue before filling, most stopping wax does not adhere well to oily surfaces.
Step 2, choose the right filler for the situation

For polished or waxed furniture with a smooth surface finish, use a wax stopping stick: press the wax into each hole with a fingertip, overfilling slightly, then scrape flush with the filling knife. The wax is soft enough to polish over without sanding. For painted or varnished timber where a harder fill is needed, use a two-part epoxy wood filler: mix a small amount, press it into the holes with the filling knife tip, allow to cure, then sand flush. For bare or oiled timber such as floorboards or beams, either approach works; epoxy gives a harder, longer-lasting fill.

Work across the affected area methodically, filling one hole at a time. Overfill each hole by approximately 0.5 mm to allow for the filler settling slightly into the hole as it sets or is compressed. Scrape flush with the filling knife held at a low angle: draw the flat of the blade across the surface, removing the proud filler without gouging the surrounding wood. Wax stopping can be scraped immediately; epoxy filler must be set and then sanded.

For epoxy-filled holes, sand the area lightly with 180-grit paper using the sanding block, then 240-grit, until the fills are flush and smooth. Reapply the surface finish, oil, wax, varnish, or paint, over the repaired area to blend the fills into the surrounding surface. Two coats of finishing oil over the whole surface gives the most seamless result on bare or oiled timber.

attrs.fix: Use a paler shade for the next set of holes and blend the two to match. On light-coloured timber, slightly pale fills are less noticeable than slightly dark ones because they tend to pick up ambient surface light rather than read as shadows.
attrs.cause: The filler colour is a shade darker than the timber.
attrs.symptom: Filled holes are visible as slightly darker dots

attrs.fix: Apply a penetrating boron-based or permethrin woodworm treatment to the entire affected piece. Filling is cosmetic only and does not address an active infestation.
attrs.cause: The infestation was not fully treated, or a new infestation has begun in an adjacent untreated section.
attrs.symptom: New holes appear in the same timber after filling

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
